# CloudHub - Full Documentation

# CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 Application Migration

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

After your Anypoint Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) upgrade is complete, you can migrate all associated
applications. Migration involves creating an equivalent CloudHub 2.0 application for every CloudHub
application, and linking them.

The current CloudHub application runs without disruption until the upgrade completes. Then, you can
use the same CloudHub URL for your CloudHub 2.0 application.

> [!NOTE] During the application upgrade process, MuleSoft doesnât provide any out-of-the-box
> entitlements. Work with your account representative to remain compliant with entitlements and
> finish the upgrade on time.

## Eligibility

To link a CloudHub application with a CloudHub 2.0 application and gradually switch traffic using
the application migration tool, first upgrade the CloudHub VPC to a CloudHub 2.0 private space by
using the [VPC upgrade tool](vpc-upgrade).

You can use the application upgrade tool only in CloudHub 2.0 private spaces that were upgraded from
a CloudHub VPC.

## Upgrade Your CloudHub Application to CloudHub 2.0 via Anypoint Platform

1.  From **Anypoint Platform**, select **Runtime Manager** > **Applications**.
2.  Select the CloudHub application to upgrade and go to **Settings**.

    If your application is eligible for the upgrade, the upgrade tooling is available on the
    **Settings** page.

3.  Click **Upgrade to CloudHub 2.0**, then click **Start Upgrade**.

    ![CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 upgrade window summarizing the application migration process and containing the Start Upgrade button.](_images/upgrade-application-window.png)

    The **Upgrade Application to CloudHub 2.0** page opens.
    - The **Application Name** field shows the inherited CloudHub application name by default.

      To use a different name for your CloudHub 2.0 application, change it by editing the field.

    - The **Upgrading From** field shows the name of the CloudHub application the new application is
      migrated from.

      You canât edit this field. The **Deployment Target** field shows the preselected private space
      for the application deployment based on the upgraded CloudHub VPC.

      You canât edit this field.

4.  For **Application File**, you can either:
    - Upload your application file: Download the application asset from the link under **Application
      File**, make the necessary changes, and upload it to deploy your new application to the
      CloudHub 2.0 private space.
    - Import file from Exchange: Import the CloudHub application you want to upgrade.

5.  In the **Ingress** tab, specify the **Endpoint** configuration:
    1.  Select a **Host** from the dropdown list.

        > [!NOTE] If youâre using the `anypoint.dns.net` endpoint without a TLS context, the
        > **Ingress** tab doesnât show it as a possible endpoint.

    2.  Select a specific subdomain from the dropdown list.
    3.  Add a specific path.
    4.  Optionally, provide a specific path rewrite.
    5.  Select whether you want to provide internal or external access to your endpoint.

6.  Follow the steps to [deploy an application to CloudHub 2.0](../cloudhub-2/ch2-deploy).

> [!WARNING] If you use a different name for the CloudHub 2.0 application, make sure you put the
> CloudHub application name in the **Ingress** tab settings in the subdomain field of the endpoint
> configuration. If you configure URLs incorrectly, traffic doesnât reach the CloudHub 2.0
> application during [traffic switching](#next-steps-after-application-upgrade).
>
> In applications with inbound traffic, the Shared Load Balancer (SLB) handles traffic switching if
> your Ingress configuration includes the default CloudHub application endpoint in the format:
> `<app_name>.<region>.cloudhub.io`. However, if that default endpoint is missing, and you have
> configured a vanity domain or an `anypointdns.net endpoint` instead, the Dedicated Load Balancer
> (DLB) switches the traffic. For more information about SLB and DLB traffic switching, see
> [Understanding the Traffic Source](#understanding-the-traffic-source).

> [!NOTE] The **Host** dropdown list shows only the domains initially added under the certificates
> of the underlying VPCâs DLB configuration. If you add a new certificate to the original VPCâs DLB
> after the application upgrade starts, add it manually under the **Domain & TLS** tab of your newly
> created private space for that domain to appear on the **Host** dropdown list.

### Considerations and Limitations

The upgrade process doesnât automatically map some configuration settings to the new CloudHub 2.0
application.

#### Application Properties

The upgrade process doesnât carry over application properties to CloudHub 2.0. Reconfigure these
properties manually for a successful upgrade:

- The upgrade process copies your CloudHub appâs properties during the CloudHub 2.0 application
  configuration. These properties donât persist unless you edit at least one property or add a new
  property. Editing a single property, or adding a new one, causes the edited or new property and
  all other copied properties to persist.
- The upgrade process copies the protected properties added during CloudHub 1.0 with `******` as
  their displayed value. Change this displayed value manually to the correct protected value within
  your CloudHub 2.0 application settings.

#### Managing Migrated Apps via Mule Maven Plugin (MMP)

To manage a migrated app using the Mule Maven plugin (MMP), copy the public URL configured in
Runtime Manager during the migration. If the endpoints specified in the `publicUrl` field of your
`pom.xml` file donât match the ones configured in the UI, the Ingress configuration is lost.

#### CloudHub Application Default Domain

When deploying a new CloudHub 2.0 application, avoid using the CloudHub default domain to prevent
issues such as traffic outages.

## Next Steps After Application Upgrade

After the upgrade process deploys your CloudHub 2.0 application, test the app by directing traffic
to it and verifying network connectivity and the app behavior.

To avoid updating your client applications that connect to CloudHub, retain the domains from
CloudHub on CloudHub 2.0. To link your applications, apply either of these options:

> [!NOTE] You can also use APIs for
> [application linking](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/amc-application-manager/minor/4.1/console/method/%231340/)
> using the `upgradeData` object while creating CloudHub 2.0 deployments, and for
> [traffic switching](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/amc-application-manager/minor/4.1/console/method/%231736/)
> using the newly introduced endpoint.

Customer-Managed

Switch the applicationâs [DNS](../cloudhub-2/ch2-config-endpoints-paths) to point to CloudHub 2.0.

MuleSoft-Managed

Carry over vanity URLs while incrementally switching percentage of traffic from CloudHub to CloudHub
2.0.

> [!IMPORTANT] Restart your DLB before initiating the traffic switching.

> [!NOTE] To switch application traffic, you must have the CloudHub Network Administrator
> permission. Without this permission, the traffic switching fails. For more information about
> assigning permissions, see
> [Permissions by Product in Anypoint Platform](../access-management/permissions-by-product).

> [!IMPORTANT] The CloudHub application must remain in STARTED state until the link between CloudHub
> and CloudHub 2.0 is successfully established. Stopping the CloudHub application before the link is
> established can cause traffic disruptions.

To gradually switch traffic from CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0:

1.  In **Runtime Manager**, go to **Finish Upgrade**.
2.  Select the percentage of traffic to direct from CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0, and click **Save
    Changes**.

    ![Traffic switching window showing the percentage slider to gradually redirect traffic from CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 and the Save Changes button](_images/traffic-switching.png)

> [!NOTE] You can roll back to CloudHub if there are failures during this stage.

### Understanding the Traffic Source

The traffic source determines which type of DNS record the process updates to manage the traffic
flow between your CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0 applications during the migration process. Understanding
this concept helps you anticipate how the system manages traffic based on your applicationâs
existing and new endpoint configurations.

CloudHub receives traffic from two main sources:

- Shared Load Balancer (SLB): This source is typically associated with the default endpoints of your
  CloudHub application. In applications with inbound traffic, the SLB handles traffic switching if
  your Ingress configuration includes the default CloudHub application endpoint in the format:
  `<app_name>.<region>.cloudhub.io`.
- Dedicated Load Balancer (DLB): This source is usually associated with vanity domains configured
  for your CloudHub application. If the default CloudHub endpoint is missing, and you have
  configured a vanity domain or an `anypointdns.net` endpoint, the DLB switches the traffic.

During traffic switching, CloudHub needs to know the traffic source to correctly switch traffic
between CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0. If you change your applicationâs endpoint configuration, the
inferred traffic source also changes. For example, if you initially have only vanity domains (DLB)
and then add a CloudHub default endpoint, CloudHub infers the traffic source as the SLB.

> [!NOTE] If your CloudHub 1.0 application has the default endpoint configured, traffic switches at
> the SLB. To switch traffic from the DLB instead, remove the CloudHub 1.0 default endpoint, apply
> changes, and then change the traffic switch percentage.

To ensure successful traffic switching, follow specific configurations depending on the type of load
balancer you want to use and the type of traffic you expect.

#### DLB with HTTPS Traffic Switching

For applications using a DLB and expecting HTTPS traffic:

1.  Enable the **Last-Mile Security** setting in the **Ingress** tab of the deployment settings for
    the CloudHub 2.0 application.
2.  Enable **Upstream TLS 1.2** or higher in your DLB settings.
3.  Make sure that vanity domains on CloudHub 2.0 match those on the CloudHub application to prevent
    dropped traffic.

#### DLB with HTTP Traffic Switching

For applications using a DLB and expecting HTTP traffic:

1.  Add a firewall rule allowing HTTP from the local private network.

> [!WARNING] The CloudHub DLB routes requests from clients to Mule apps deployed within the VPC. The
> DLB sends requests to a Mule application and uses the HTTP Host header to find the certificate
> endpoint that matches the request. Then, the DLB uses the URL mapping rules for that endpoint to
> send the request to the Mule application. If the request doesnât match any certificate configured
> on the DLB, the DLB uses the "default" certificate endpoint to match the request against its URL
> mapping rules. During traffic switching, CloudHub 2.0 doesnât contain mapping rules for this
> unmatched host within the default certificate.

#### SLB with HTTPS Traffic Switching

For applications using an SLB and expecting HTTPS traffic:

- Enable the **Last-Mile Security** setting in the **Ingress** tab of the deployment settings for
  the CloudHub 2.0 application.

#### SLB with HTTP Traffic Switching

For applications using an SLB and expecting HTTP traffic:

1.  Enable the **Accept HTTP** option within the **Advanced** tab of your private space settings.
2.  Add a firewall rule allowing HTTP from any source.

### Managing API Policies During Traffic Switching

When you apply API policies to your new application, they begin with a clean internal status.

#### Policies Unchanged by the App Migration

These policies donât rely on internal status and their behavior remains consistent:

- http-basic-authentication
- cors
- client id enforcement
- Header injection
- Header removal
- ip-allowlist
- ip-blocklist
- ldap-authentication
- message-logging
- json-threat-protection
- xml-threat-protection

#### Policies Affected by Internal Status (Caching)

These policies rely on internal status to cache data:

- external-oauth2-access-token-enforcement
- openam-access-token-enforcement
- openidconnect-access-token-enforcement
- pingfederate-access-token-enforcement
- mule-http-caching-policy
- jwt-validation

When transitioning to the new application, these policies can either trigger additional requests to
the origin servers or have different cached values depending on which application receives the
requests.

#### Policies Affected by Internal Status (Request Counting)

These policies track the number of processed requests and can sometimes exhibit unexpected behavior
during the transition to the new application:

- rate-limiting
- rate-limiting-sla-based
- spike-control

If thereâs a hard change in traffic from one application to the other, policy counters behave as if
they were reset. In a balanced round-robin traffic distribution, the effective number of requests
can sometimes double because each application maintains an independent counting mechanism.

During the CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 application migration, traffic gradually shifts, and both
applications are active simultaneously. If your API Manager policies are integrated with your
original CloudHub application, the system canât share or migrate these configurations in real time.
To maintain consistent policy enforcement, configure equivalent policies on your CloudHub 2.0
applicationâs API instance during traffic switching.

Autodiscovery connects a deployed Mule application with an API defined in API Manager. Throughout
the migration, both your CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0 applications link to the same API.

## Complete the Application Upgrade

After traffic fully redirects to the CloudHub 2.0 application, stop your CloudHub application within
30 days and mark the upgrade as complete.

If you change your CloudHub application after it stops, the changes arenât replicated in your new
CloudHub 2.0 application. Make any required changes to the CloudHub 2.0 application settings.

After your CloudHub application stops, delete it within 30 days.

> [!NOTE] After the CloudHub application stops, traffic switches to the linked CloudHub 2.0
> application. To roll back the application upgrade, restart your CloudHub application and configure
> the traffic switching percentage. After you delete the CloudHub application, the migration is
> complete. You then canât create another CloudHub application with the same application name.

Complete or delete all CloudHub applications in a given upgraded VPC to release entitlements.

## Perform DLB Failover

After the VPC upgrade and CloudHub application migration to CloudHub 2.0, perform a DLB failover to
retain domains during the transition from CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0. This ensures client application
compatibility.

To perform a DLB failover:

1.  For each DLB linked to your upgraded VPC, go to the DLB settings page and click **Failover to
    CloudHub 2.0**.

    This fails over the `anypointdns.net` domain to CloudHub 2.0, removes any additional DLB hop,
    and redirects traffic to your new CloudHub 2.0 private space URL.

2.  After the traffic failover completes, stop and delete the CloudHub DLB linked to the VPC.

> [!NOTE] If there are failures during this stage, restart the DLB to point `anypointdns.net` to
> CloudHub while the DLB is in a stopped state. After you delete the DLB, you canât roll back to
> CloudHub.

For the final steps after VPC upgrade and CloudHub application migration, see
[CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 Migration Completion](ch-ch2-migration-completion).

## See Also

- [CloudHub VPC to CloudHub 2.0 Private Space Upgrade](vpc-upgrade)
- [CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 Migration Completion](ch-ch2-migration-completion)
- [Deploying Apps to Private Spaces](../cloudhub-2/ch2-deploy-private-space)
---

# Autoscaling in CloudHub

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

> [!NOTE] To use the autoscaling feature, your organization must have an Enterprise License
> Agreement (ELA). Entitlement is approved based on qualified use cases. If you are interested in
> this feature, contact your account representative.
>
> Autoscaling isnât available on CloudHub for Usage-based Pricing (UBP) organizations.

CloudHub allows you to make the processing power behind your applications responsive to changes in
their usage. You can define policies that respond to CPU or memory usage thresholds by scaling up or
scaling down the processing resources used by an application.

## Configure Autoscaling

1.  Click the **Applications** tab of Runtime Manager.
2.  Select an application deployed to CloudHub to open its panel.
3.  In the panel, click **Manage Application**.
4.  In the **Settings** section, select the **Autoscaling** tab.  
    You can see all existing autoscaling policies, activate or deactivate them, create or delete
    them, and create associated
    [Alerts on Runtime Manager](../runtime-manager/alerts-on-runtime-manager).

## Create a Policy

To create a policy, click the **Create Policy** button, then configure the fields to set your policy
up.

![policy](_images/autoscale-create-policy.png)

Changes in worker size or number are triggered when a policy meets its requirements. Policies can be
based either upon CPU or Memory usage, and they can either modify worker numbers or worker size. All
policies include a scale up and a scale down condition.

![form](_images/policy-form.png)

1.  Provide a **Name** for your policy.
2.  Determine if the scaling will be based on **Memory or CPU usage**.

    > [!NOTE] The percentage shown is the percentage of total memory or CPU allocated for the JVM
    > thatâs being used. This includes the memory being consumed by the JVM itself.

3.  Determine the **Rules** for your scaling. You must provide the following values for both the
    upscale and the downscale conditions:
    1.  A usage **percentage**
    2.  A period of **time** throughout which this level must be sustained before the policy is
        applied
    3.  A **cool-down period** that blocks this and other policies from affecting scaling from being
        applied

4.  Define what **action** will be performed when the policy is applied:
    1.  If it will affect the **number** of workers or the **size** of them
    2.  Set a **maximum** and **minimum** value to fluctuate in between

> [!NOTE] Maximum number of workers is 4, and maximum worker size is 16 vCores for the Autoscaling
> policy.

## Scaling Considerations

All policies scale your application one step at a time. To scale up to meet a pronounced spike that
requires scaling numerous steps, each step takes CloudHub the sum of the policy evaluation time plus
the cool-down period.

You can apply only one autoscaling policy per application.

The maximum amount and size of workers that you have available is limited by the characteristics of
your account. If multiple applications running in your account compete for limited resources, they
are allocated in a first-come, first-served manner.

Autoscaling fails if your org reaches its vCore limit because there are no available resources to
provision new workers. To continue, increase your orgâs vCore limit or stop unused apps to free up
vCores.

## Edit an Existing Policy

To edit an existing policy, open the **Policies** tab. Click the policy name and you will access the
same menu that is used to create a new policy.

## Deactivate a Policy

To deactivate a running policy, open the policies tab and flip the switch in its corresponding
**Status** field:

![enable](_images/enable-policy.png)

## Delete a Policy

To delete an existing policy, go to the policies tab, then click its corresponding delete icon.

## Set an Alert and View Autoscaling History

Two specific types of Alerts can be set up for applications on CloudHub that warn you whenever a
worker autoscaling event occurs. One alerts you about scale ups and the other about scale downs.

With this alert set, emails will be automatically sent to the addresses you provide whenever such an
event occurs.

You can also see a full history of all of the times that the alert has been triggered. To see this,
from the **Alerts** page, click on an alert name to open its corresponding panel:

![history](_images/alerts-history-autoscale.png)

## See Also

- [Alerts on Runtime Manager](../runtime-manager/alerts-on-runtime-manager)
- [Manage Deployed Applications](../runtime-manager/managing-deployed-applications)
- [CloudHub](./)
- [Deployment Options](../runtime-manager/deployment-strategies)
- [Clustering](cloudhub-fabric)
- [Anypoint Platform Command-Line Interface](../anypoint-cli/latest/)
- [Secure Application Properties](secure-application-properties)
- [Virtual Private Cloud](virtual-private-cloud)
---

# CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 Migration Completion

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

After completing the VPC upgrade and application migration phases, complete the CloudHub to CloudHub
2.0 migration process and release associated entitlements within the recommended time frame:

## Mark Upgrade as Complete

> [!WARNING] Marking the upgrade as complete is an irreversible action. This action deletes the
> original VPC, stopped DLBs, and all CloudHub 1.0 applications marked as upgraded. All traffic then
> flows through the CloudHub 2.0 private space.

Marking the upgrade as complete finishes the entire VPC upgrade process. Go to the **Upgrade** tab
and click **Mark Upgrade Complete**.

![*Mark Upgrade Complete* button within the Upgrade tab](_images/mark-upgrade-complete.png)

After you mark the upgrade complete, CloudHub 2.0 replaces all CloudHub resources and CloudHub
assets become immutable. Perform any required changes on CloudHub 2.0.

## Entitlements for VPCs, Private Spaces, and Network Connections (VPNs/TGW)

After you complete the VPC upgrade and bring over any existing TGW and VPN network connections, your
old and new infrastructure count separately against your usage. Therefore, if your organization uses
all purchased quota, your usage can exceed the allowed quota until you move all your applications to
CloudHub 2.0 and decommission your old VPC.

Work with your account representative to plan for additional entitlements, and decommission your
CloudHub infrastructure (VPCs and associated DLBs) within six months after the upgrade to avoid
interruptions when trying to create or edit VPCs, private spaces, or network connections.

> [!NOTE] Deleting the upgraded VPC after all applications and their associated DLBs are removed
> doesnât affect your CloudHub 2.0 Private Spaces or CloudHub 2.0 applications.

## See Also

- [CloudHub VPC to CloudHub 2.0 Private Space Upgrade](vpc-upgrade)
- [CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 Application Migration](app-migration)
---

# CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 Migration Configuration

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Migrate your CloudHub configurations to CloudHub 2.0 by following critical steps, handling
customizations, and ensuring all configuration dependencies resolve correctly. This process reduces
risks and keeps your systems compliant and efficient.

## Understand the Differences

Identify critical architectural differences and configuration gaps between ClouHub and CloudHub 2.0.
Review deployment models and environmental dependencies that affect custom configurations. Use this
information to accurately scope the migration effort and reduce incompatibility risk during upgrade
planning.

### Infrastructure and Networking Differences

Architecture

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>The CloudHub architecture includes two major components: Anypoint Platform services and a multitenant cloud of virtual machines distributed across global regions where your applications run. Individual instances of an application are called workers. CloudHub uses a VM-based infrastructure.</p></td><td><p>The CloudHub 2.0 architecture includes two major components: Anypoint Platform services and a multitenant cloud of containerized replicas distributed across global regions where your applications run. Individual instances of an application are called replicas. CloudHub 2.0 uses a container-based infrastructure.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span><img src="_images/ch1-architecture-diagram-new.png" alt="Diagram showing CloudHub architecture"></span></p></td><td><p><span><img src="_images/ch2-architecture-diagram-new.png" alt="Diagram showing CloudHub 2.0 architecture"></span></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Private Network

The Private Network extends your corporate network and helps CloudHub workers and CloudHub 2.0
replicas to access resources behind the corporate firewall. Creating a private network differs
between CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>A <a href="virtual-private-cloud">Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)</a> is a virtual, private, and isolated cloud network segment for hosting CloudHub workers.</p></td><td><p>A private space is a virtual, private, and isolated logical space in CloudHub 2.0 for running Mule apps.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Static IPs are managed per application, not at the VPC level. Turn on <strong>Static IPs</strong> for individual apps to receive their own set of egress static IPs.</p></td><td><p>Static IPs are provisioned at the private space level. All applications in the same private space share these outbound static IPs.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub provides a Shared Load Balancer (SLB) by default for all apps. The Dedicated Load Balancer (DLB) is optional, and you create and configure it within the VPC.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 automatically provisions a fully managed ingress load balancer for the private space.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>TLS/SSL certificates require manual configuration. The SLB uses the platformâs wildcard certificate. For custom domains, create a DLB and upload PEM-encoded certificates and private keys.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 automatically creates a default TLS context for the private space with wildcard certificates for both internal and public endpoints. You can configure custom vanity domains with your own TLS context for both internal and external endpoints.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Connectivity Methods

The private network connects to the corporate network using these connectivity methods:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><ul><li><p>VPC peering (AWS VPC to Mule VPC)</p><div><p>Requires configuration. Contact the MuleSoft Support team to enable.</p></div></li><li><p>AWS Direct Connect + Requires configuration. Contact the MuleSoft Support team to enable.</p></li><li><p>Transit Gateway Attachments</p></li><li><p>VPN tunnel/IPsec tunnel</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>VPN tunnel</p></li><li><p>Transit Gateway Attachments</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Contact the MuleSoft Support team to enable VPC peering and AWS Direct Connect.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 supports Transit Gateway or VPN tunnel.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Firewall Rules

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub Ingress (Inbound) Rules</th><th>CloudHub 2.0 Ingress (Inbound) Rules</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CloudHub adds firewall rules automatically when you create a VPC.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 adds firewall rules automatically when you create a private space.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>By default, all traffic to a VPC is blocked unless explicitly allowed by a firewall rule. However, CloudHub automatically creates four default inbound firewall rules when you create a VPC.</p></td><td><p>Traffic to a private space is blocked unless explicitly allowed by a firewall rule.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>CloudHub creates four default inbound rules automatically:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Two rules to allow inbound connections from within your local Anypoint VPC through ports 8091 and 8092.</p><div><ul><li><p>Specifically for DLB interactions.</p></li></ul></div></li><li><p>Two rules allow inbound connections from anywhere through ports 8081 and 8082.</p><div><ul><li><p>Specifically for SLB interactions.</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>CloudHub 2.0 creates two default inbound rules automatically:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Two rules to allow inbound connections from any host:</p><div><ul><li><p>HTTPS: port 443</p></li><li><p>HTTP: port 80</p></li></ul></div></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub supports HTTP, HTTPS, and TCP protocols. The Dedicated Load Balancer (DLB) also supports WebSockets (ws and wss).</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 supports HTTP, HTTPS, and TCP protocols. The upgrade tooling does not support TCP traffic migration yet.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>The maximum number of firewall rules is approximately 35 per VPC, depending on the number of rules CloudHub requires.</p></td><td><p>The maximum number of inbound firewall rules is 300.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><colgroup><col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Egress (Outbound) Rules</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CloudHub doesnât support egress firewall rules. It allows all outbound traffic by default.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 creates two default outbound rules automatically. You can configure additional rules and application-level egress rules for granular control. The maximum number of outbound firewall rules is 300.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>CloudHub 2.0 adds two egress rules by default. You can delete these rules if you want to block all outbound traffic for security reasons:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>TCP: All ports (to anywhere)</p></li><li><p>HTTPS: 443 (to anywhere)</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><colgroup><col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub 2.0 Application-Level Egress Rules</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 supports egress firewall rules at the application level. Application-level egress rules are independent of private-space-level firewall rules. See <a href="../cloudhub-2/ps-config-app-level-egress">Configuring Application-Level Egress Rules</a>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Turn on application-level egress rules in Runtime Manager.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>In CloudHub 2.0, egress traffic passes through both application-level control (enforced first) and private-space-level control (enforced after application-level control).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Apply application-level egress rules when you deploy your application. See <a href="../cloudhub-2/ps-config-app-level-egress#apply-application-level-egress-rules-when-deploying-your-applications">Apply Application-Level Egress Rules</a>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>You can configure application-level egress connections, such as:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Anypoint MQ</p></li><li><p>Object Store v2</p></li><li><p>API Manager</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

Static IP Addresses

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CloudHub supports static IP addresses at the application level.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 supports static IP addresses at the private space level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>If you turn on <strong>Static IPs</strong> for an application, CloudHub provides outbound static IPs.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 provides inbound and outbound static IPs at the private space level.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Applications deployed to the same VPC donât automatically share static IPs. If you turn on <strong>Static IPs</strong> for an application, it receives its own set of outbound static IPs. Inbound static IPs are available only through a DLB, and all applications behind the same DLB share them.</p></td><td><p>Applications deployed to the same private space use the same set of inbound and outbound static IPs. To have unique static IPs per application, use a different private space.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Load Balancing

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>CloudHub provides two types of load balancing:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Shared Load Balancer (SLB): CloudHub provides it by default for all apps.</p></li><li><p>Dedicated Load Balancer (DLB): You create it within the VPC.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 automatically provides a fully managed ingress load balancer for each private space with a simplified architecture that removes the need to select or create load balancers.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub load balancers provide round-robin load distribution across workers.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 provides a fully managed ingress load balancer for each private space that automatically balances inbound traffic across multiple application replicas. The ingress load balancer auto-scales to accommodate traffic. You can configure this load balancer to meet your specific requirements.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub doesnât support load balancer logs for the DLB. The SLB has no log access.</p></td><td><p>Download ingress traffic logs from Runtime Manager for full visibility into ingress traffic and troubleshooting.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>You can assign up to four load balancer units (up to eight workers total) to a Dedicated Load Balancer (DLB) for high availability and load distribution. The Shared Load Balancer (SLB) provides fully managed high availability with no customer configuration required.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 automatically distributes application replicas across two or more availability zones (AZs) for built-in high availability and fault tolerance. If you use the <a href="vpc-upgrade">VPC upgrade tool</a>, the private space inherits the same number of AZs as the original CloudHub VPC.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Domains and TLS Context

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>SSL termination happens at the DLB layer.</p></td><td><p>SSL termination happens at the ingress load balancer layer.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>The communication between the DLB and the Mule application is internal to the VPC.</p></td><td><p>Applications inside a private space communicate using the ingress load balancer via the internal endpoint. This depends on the application protocol.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Supported protocols:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><a href="https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=004205269&amp;type=1">TLS 1.1</a> (for existing DLBs until January 31, 2026)</p></li><li><p>TLS 1.2</p></li><li><p><a href="https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=001116412&amp;type=1">TLS 1.3</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Supported protocols:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>TLS 1.2</p></li><li><p>TLS 1.3</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td></td><td><p>An application can have multiple endpoints, depending on âthe domain and TLS context configuration.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Mutual TLS

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CloudHub doesnât provide native mutual TLS (mTLS) capability. The Dedicated Load Balancer (DLB) handles mTLS at the infrastructure level, performing client certificate validation and forwarding certificate information to applications via HTTP headers.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 provides native mTLS with TLS contexts and truststores. Configure mTLS at the private space level with centralized management. CloudHub 2.0 also supports forward SSL, which forwards client certificate information to applications via HTTP headers.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub supports limited Certificate Authority (CA) certificates through the DLB. It accepts certificate files in PEM format only.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 provides dynamic certificate management, application-level <a href="../cloudhub-2/ps-config-domains#truststore">truststore</a> access, and in-place updates without redeployment. Upload truststore files in PEM and JKS formats.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

URL Rewriting

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Implement URL rewriting at the DLB level using mapping rules. Configure a DLB to use this feature.</p></td><td><p>Implement URL rewrite at the application level using domains and endpoints for a more flexible, per-app configuration.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Internal Application Communication

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>The internal worker hostname <code>mule-worker-internal-&lt;app&gt;.&lt;region&gt;.cloudhub.io</code> resolves to the Mule workerâs internal IP address for VPC-internal communication.</p></td><td><div><div><p>Use one of these three options:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Internal endpoint: <code>internal-&lt;app&gt;-&lt;uniq-id&gt;.&lt;space-id&gt;.&lt;region&gt;.cloudhub.io.</code></p></li><li><p>Custom internal endpoint: CNAME pointing to the internal endpoint. You can configure custom TLS contexts for internal endpoints that are only accessible via the private network.</p></li><li><p>Kubernetes service address: Direct pod-to-pod communication. This works only for applications in the same namespace (environment).</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Application Differences

Persistent VM Queues Vs. Anypoint MQ

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CloudHub persistent queues store VM queue messages externally to the application. Use this feature mainly with VM components. Turn on CloudHub persistent queues on a per-application basis.</p></td><td><p><a href="../mq/">Anypoint MQ</a> provides persistent message storage in cloud infrastructure, eliminating VM-dependent queue management. Anypoint MQ provides asynchronous message processing, event-driven architectures, and cross-application communication. Configure Anypoint MQ on a per-application basis with automatic scaling and high availability.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Logging

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>During deployment, CloudHub automatically replaces the application archiveâs <code>log4j2.xml</code> file with a predefined template. CloudHub uses the original <code>log4j2.xml</code> file only when you disable CloudHub logs.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 merges the <code>log4j2.xml</code> configuration file from the application archive with predefined appenders. These appenders send logs directly to Runtime Manager and Anypoint Monitoring, preserving custom appenders while enabling platform integration.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Download the application and runtime logs separately.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 merges application and runtime logs into a single log stream. To find runtime logs, download the application log and check for runtime-related messages.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub stores logs in the region where it was first deployed. If you move the app to another region, the logs remain stored in the initial deployment region. To reset this log retention configuration, delete and re-create the app.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 stores logs in the region where the application is currently deployed. You canât move the app to a different region once deployed. To reset this log retention configuration, delete and re-create the app.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub has limited control over log forwarding.</p></td><td><div><div><p>If you deselect <strong>Forward application logs to Anypoint Platform</strong> within the app configuration in Runtime Manager:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>CloudHub 2.0 doesnât forward application logs to Runtime Manager or Anypoint Monitoring.</p></li><li><p>The custom log appenders configured in your application continue to function normally (excluding file-based appenders).</p></li></ul></div><div><p>This provides more granular control over platform log forwarding.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>For more information, see <a href="https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=001117963&amp;type=1">CloudHub 1.0 Logging FAQ</a></p></td><td><p>For more information, see <a href="https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=001119527&amp;type=1">CloudHub 2.0 Logging FAQ</a></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

Deployment Configuration

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Use maven and <code>pom.xml</code> configuration to deploy applications to CloudHub. See <a href="../mule-runtime/latest/deploy-to-cloudhub">Deploy Applications to CloudHub Using the Mule Maven Plugin</a>.</p></td><td><div><div><p>App deployment with maven and <code>pom.xml</code> configuration takes two steps:</p></div><div><ol><li><p>Publish the artifact to Anypoint Exchange (<code>mvn clean deploy</code>).</p></li><li><p>Deploy the artifact to CloudHub 2.0 (<code>mvn clean deploy</code>, <code>-DmuleDeploy</code>).</p></li></ol></div><div><p>For more information, see <a href="../mule-runtime/latest/deploy-to-cloudhub-2">Deploy Applications to CloudHub 2.0 Using the Mule Maven Plugin</a>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>The maximum size of a Mule application (ZIP or JAR) file is 200 MB.</p></td><td><p>The maximum size of a Mule application JAR file is 350 MB.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Names are unique per control plane.</p></td><td><div><div><p>Names are unique per private space. CloudHub 2.0 adds a six-character unique ID to the application name that you specify in the public endpoint URL: <code>Myapp-uniq-id.shard.region.cloudhub.io</code></p></div><div><ul><li><p><code>uniq-id</code>: is a six-digit value appended to the app name to ensure uniqueness.</p></li><li><p><code>shard</code>: is a six-digit value associated with the space (private or shared) the app is deployed to.</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>The DLB supports a maximum HTTP POST (payload) size of 200 MB.</p></td><td><p>The maximum HTTP request size is 1 GB.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

vCore Sizes

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>See <a href="cloudhub-architecture#cloudhub-workers">CloudHub Workers</a>.</p></td><td><p>See <a href="../cloudhub-2/ch2-architecture#cloudhub-2-replicas">CloudHub 2.0 Replicas</a>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

CloudHub 2.0 provides a more granular resource capacity for applications. You can assign specific
vCore increments (including 0.5, 1.5, 2.5, 3, 3.5, and 4). This granularity helps match processing
needs precisely and avoids forcing a jump from 0.2 to 1 vCores. Replicas with fewer than 1.0 vCores
offer limited CPU and I/O suitable for smaller workloads, while those with one or more vCores
provide performance consistency.

Worker Vs. Replica

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>A worker in CloudHub is a dedicated Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) virtual machine instance that runs your Mule application. It functions as a full, independent virtual server with its own operating system, memory, CPU resources, and isolated runtime environment.</p></td><td><p>A replica in CloudHub 2.0 is a dedicated, containerized instance of Mule runtime engine that runs your integration application. Each replica provides complete isolation between applications with its own isolated environment, allocated memory (heap and total), CPU resources (vCores), and storage capacity.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>If you select <strong>Automatically restart application when not responding</strong>, CloudHub monitors the worker and automatically restarts the application if necessary.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 provides self-healing capabilities that automatically recover applications from both Mule process failures and underlying infrastructure failures. If either the Mule process or the infrastructure fails, CloudHub 2.0 automatically restarts the application on healthy infrastructure without your intervention. Application crash recovery is enabled by default.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span><img src="_images/worker-logical-rep.png" alt="Diagram showing the logic of a worker" width="500" height="300"></span></p></td><td><p><span><img src="_images/replica-logical-rep.png" alt="Diagram showing the logic of a replica" width="500" height="300"></span></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

High Availability with Clustering

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CloudHub doesnât support Mule clustering. You can run multiple workers for scale and High Availability (HA), but theyâre independent runtimes without cluster semantics. Thereâs no distributed in-memory state or cluster-aware components.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 supports Mule clustering. Multiple replicas of the same app form a managed cluster, enabling cluster-aware runtime behavior. This includes shared memory and distributed locking across runtime replicas, with a single primary replica running the application logic and secondary replica(s) available for high-availability if the primary goes down.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub scales applications by deploying multiple workers (up to eight) for each application. The SLB automatically distributes traffic across these workers. For applications that need shared state, store that state externally (for example, in a database or Object Store). Workers canât share in-memory state.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 scales applications by deploying multiple replicas (up to sixteen) for each application. The platformâs cluster mode coordinates behavior across these replicas automatically. For applications with stateful requirements, use Muleâs native clustered features (such as clustered object stores and clustered caches) to maintain state across replicas.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub achieves HA with two or more workers. It supports availability across AZs, and the platform handles failover.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 provides fully managed HA. The platform automatically spreads replicas across two or more AZs, with round-robin HTTP load balancing and cluster-aware recovery. If you use the <a href="vpc-upgrade">VPC upgrade tool</a>, the private space inherits the same number of AZs as the original CloudHub VPC.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>CloudHub monitors workers and automatically performs restarts. Blue-green deployment at the DLB or SLB layer provides seamless cutover, but this process lacks cluster draining and coordination within the runtime.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 provides orchestrated restarts and rolling updates across replicas. Cluster-aware coordination minimizes impact during upgrades and failures.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>You manage worker counts and rely on external stores for shared state.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 provides fully managed clustering. Select replica counts, and the platform handles cluster formation, health, placement, and recovery.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Runtime Manager Differences

Alerts

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>CloudHub</th><th>CloudHub 2.0</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>CloudHub triggers custom alerts when your application sends notifications to Runtime Manager using Anypoint Connector for CloudHub (CloudHub Connector). For more information, see <a href="custom-application-alerts">Custom Application Alerts</a>.</p></td><td><p>CloudHub 2.0 enables application deployment success and failure alerts. For custom alerting, CloudHub 2.0 leverages Anypoint Monitoring Alerts For more information, see <a href="../cloudhub-2/ch2-config-app-alerts">Configuring Application Alerts</a>.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Migrate Your CloudHub Applications to CloudHub 2.0

Migrate your CloudHub applications to CloudHub 2.0 by gathering reusable artifacts, creating private
spaces, and updating application configurations. Collect CIDR blocks, VPN connections, SSL
certificates, and firewall rules from your existing CloudHub environment. Update Mule runtime
versions to a supported runtime version. If you are using persistent queues and CloudHub connectors,
modify application code to remove them. Deploy applications using the two-step process through
Anypoint Exchange and configure egress firewall rules for Mule services access.

### Before You Begin

Gather the required information to set up a private space and use your CloudHub reusable artifacts
to configure the CloudHub 2.0 environment:

- CIDR block information

  If you require both CloudHub and CloudHub 2.0 to exist at the same time on the same network, use a
  new CIDR block for CloudHub 2.0.

- VPN connection information
- SSL certificates
- DNS server information
- Ingress firewall rules
- Mule application code

For more information, see
[Information Required to Set Up a Private Space](../cloudhub-2/ps-gather-setup-info#setup-info-private-space).

### Create Your Private Space

- If youâre using DLB, configure custom TLS certificates in your private space.
- Create and configure your CloudHub 2.0 private space using one of these methods:
  - [CloudHub 2.0 API](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/cloudhub-20-api/)
  - Anypoint Runtime Manager

    See [Create a Private Space via Runtime Manager](../cloudhub-2/ps-create-configure).

### Update the Mule Runtime Version

CloudHub 2.0 supports Mule runtime versions 4.3.x and later. Upgrade to a supported Mule runtime
version.

### Update the Mule Application Code

- Update the applicationâs `pom.xml` with CloudHub 2.0 deployment configuration. See
  [Deploy to CloudHub 2.0](../mule-runtime/latest/deploy-to-cloudhub-2).
- Update the applicationâs `log4j2.xml` with log forwarding options if needed. See
  [Integrate with Your Logging System Using Log4j](../cloudhub-2/ch2-integrate-log-system).
- Remove persistent queues and implement AnypointMQ.
- Remove CloudHub Connector and replace with custom logic.

### Deploy your Application

- If youâreusing DLB, configure application mapping rules via path and path rewrite configurations
  for endpoints in the application deployment.
- Publish your application to [Anypoint Exchange](../exchange/).
- Deploy your application to your CloudHub 2.0 private space. See
  [Deploy to a Private Space](../cloudhub-2/ch2-deploy-private-space).
- Configure required egress firewall rules to access other Mule services, such as API Manager,
  ObjectStore, and Anypoint MQ. See
  [Configure an Application-Level Egress](../cloudhub-2/ps-config-app-level-egress#apply-application-level-egress-rules-when-deploying-your-applications).
- Update CI/CD scripts to support the two-step deployment process.

### Review CloudHub 2.0 Limitations

Before finalizing the migration, review the [CloudHub 2.0 Limitations](../cloudhub-2/ch2-limits).

## See Also

- [CloudHub 2.0 Overview](../cloudhub-2/)
- [CloudHub 2.0 for CloudHub 1.0 Users](../cloudhub-2/ch2-comparison)
- [Features of CloudHub 2.0](../cloudhub-2/ch2-features)
- [Limits in CloudHub 2.0](../cloudhub-2/ch2-limits)
- [CloudHub VPC to CloudHub 2.0 Private Space Upgrade](vpc-upgrade)
- [CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 Application Migration](app-migration)
- [CloudHub to CloudHub 2.0 Migration Completion](ch-ch2-migration-completion)
- [CloudHub 2.0 Architecture](../cloudhub-2/ch2-architecture)
- [CloudHub 2.0 Networking Architecture](../cloudhub-2/ch2-networking-guide)
---

# Generate Apps Diagnostics and Analyze with Einstein

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can generate diagnostics for your CloudHub apps and run an AI-powered analysis with Einstein.
Use one of these options:

- [Download diagnostics from Runtime Manager](#download-diagnostics)
- [Generate diagnostics with MuleSoft Diagnostics Agent](#run-the-mulesoft-diagnostics-agent)

## Understanding Diagnostics

Diagnostics display the Java thread dump of each CloudHub worker in each deployment. These indicate
the current state of the server, which is helpful for debugging and support. CloudHub diagnostics
include the Diagnostic Information Analysis File (DIAF).

The DIAF is a structured, standardized plain-text file. It centralizes diagnostic data and organizes
it for improved automated and manual analysis. The DIAF captures the state of events, operations,
and critical application data. See
[Mule Troubleshooting Plugin](../mule-runtime/latest/mule-troubleshooting-plugin).

DIAF is available for applications running on these Mule versions:

- 4.6.23 and later 4.6.x versions
- 4.9.10 and later 4.9.x versions
- 4.10.x and later

Without DIAF, CloudHub gives you a text file:
`_appName_-instance-_instanceId_-_workerNumber_-diag.txt`. When DIAF is included, you get a `.zip`
file instead. Open the file in a text editor or unzip the archive as needed.

## Download Diagnostics from Runtime Manager

To download diagnostics from Runtime Manager:

1.  In the **Deployments** section, select the date and worker for which you want to download
    diagnostics.
2.  Click the download icon for the worker.
3.  Select **Diagnostics**.

To download a diagnostics file directly, use this URL:

`api/v2/organizations/{{orgId}}/environments/{{envId}}/applications/{{appName}}/instances/{{instanceId}}/diagnostics`

### Analyze Diagnostics with Einstein

Before analyzing diagnostics with Einstein:

- Connect your MuleSoft organization to a Salesforce account.
- Enable Agentforce for your organization. See
  [Enable Agentforce for Your Anypoint Platform Organization](../access-management/enabling-agentforce#enable-einstein-anypoint).
- Enable Einstein entitlement for your Anypoint Platform organization. See
  [Enable Einstein Entitlement and Routing in Anypoint Platform](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=platform.mulesoft_enable_einstein_anypoint_platform.htm&type=5).
- Verify that you have either the Mule Developer Generative AI User or Organization Administrator
  role.
- Update your Mule runtime to 4.6.23 LTS, 4.9.10 LTS, 4.10 Edge, or later.
- Verify that your Mule application is running.

### Run Analyze with Einstein

To run and download the Einstein analysis:

1.  From Anypoint Platform, select **Runtime Manager** > **Applications**.
2.  Select the app name.
3.  In the navigation menu, select **Logs**.
4.  In the **Deployments** section, select the date and worker for which you want to run the
    analysis.
5.  Click the download icon for the worker and select **Analyze with Einstein**.
6.  After the process completes, click the download icon to download an `.html` file containing the
    analysis.

## Generate Diagnostics with MuleSoft Diagnostics Agent

On the Runtime Manager **Diagnostics** page, run the MuleSoft Diagnostics Agent to generate
diagnostics on demand for a chosen instance. Download the diagnostics for manual inspection, or
**Analyze with Einstein** (or both).

1.  From Anypoint Platform, select **Runtime Manager** > **Applications**.
2.  Select the app name.
3.  In the navigation menu, select **Diagnostics**.
4.  Select an active instance from the dropdown list.
5.  Click the **Analyze & Troubleshoot** dropdown. You have these options:
    - Select **Generate Mule Diagnostics** to generate and download the diagnostics for manual
      inspection.
      1.  Click **Download** to download the Mule diagnostics files.

    - Select **Analyze with Einstein** to generate the Mule diagnostics and run the analysis
      simultaneously.
      1.  Click **View Analysis** to open the analysis page.

### Generate Mule Diagnostics

When you choose **Generate Mule Diagnostics** and click **Download**, you get a `.zip` file. The
archive contains:

Mule Thread Dumps

A thread dump is a snapshot of all the threads in an instance that shows the current state of the
server.

Diagnostic Information Analysis File (DIAF)

The DIAF is a structured, standardized plain-text file. It centralizes diagnostic data and organizes
it for improved automated and manual analysis. The DIAF captures the state of events, operations,
and critical application data. See
[Mule Troubleshooting Plugin](../mule-runtime/latest/mule-troubleshooting-plugin).

> [!NOTE] The DIAF doesnât replace application logs.

## Considerations and Limitations

The MuleSoft diagnostics agent uses Einstein generative AI. Limits for running analyses apply per
application and per organization:

- 10 analyses per application, per hour
- 50 analyses per organization, per hour
- 50 analyses per application, per day
- 200 analyses per organization, per day

CloudHub retains Mule diagnostics for 30 days or up to 150 MB.

## See Also

- [CloudHub API Reference](cloudhub-api-reference#diagnostics)
- [Understanding Diagnostic Information Analysis File (DIAF)](../mule-runtime/latest/mule-troubleshooting-plugin#understanding-diagnostic-information-analysis-file-diaf)
- [View Log Data](viewing-log-data)
- [Export Data to External Analytics Tools](../runtime-manager/sending-data-from-arm-to-external-analytics-software)
- [Monitor Applications and Servers](../runtime-manager/monitoring)
- [Deployment Options](../runtime-manager/deployment-strategies)
---

# CloudHub API Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

These tables list the most commonly used CloudHub Application Programming Interface (API) endpoints
grouped by category. All endpoints use the base URL `https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/cloudhub/api`.

For full details on request parameters, payloads, and responses, see the
[interactive API reference on Exchange](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/cloudhub-api/).

## Applications

Use these endpoints to deploy, manage, and delete Mule applications running on CloudHub.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Method</th><th>Endpoint</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/applications</code></p></td><td><p>List all applications.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/applications</code></p></td><td><p>Create and deploy a new application.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/applications/{domain}</code></p></td><td><p>Get details for a specific application.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>PUT</code></p></td><td><p><code>/applications/{domain}</code></p></td><td><p>Update application metadata (workers, runtime version, properties).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>DELETE</code></p></td><td><p><code>/applications/{domain}</code></p></td><td><p>Delete an application.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For more information, see [Manage Applications on CloudHub](managing-applications-on-cloudhub).

## Logs

Use these endpoints to search and download application log entries.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Method</th><th>Endpoint</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/logs</code></p></td><td><p>Search application log entries.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/deployments/{deploymentId}/logs</code></p></td><td><p>Retrieve logs for a specific deployment.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/instances/{instanceId}/logs</code></p></td><td><p>Retrieve logs for a specific instance.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/instances/{instanceId}/log-file</code></p></td><td><p>Download the log file for a specific instance.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Notifications and Alerts

Use these endpoints to manage notifications and configure alerts for your CloudHub applications.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Method</th><th>Endpoint</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/notifications</code></p></td><td><p>List notifications.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/notifications</code></p></td><td><p>Create a notification.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>PUT</code></p></td><td><p><code>/notifications/{notificationId}</code></p></td><td><p>Mark a notification as read or unread.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/alerts</code></p></td><td><p>List alerts for an application.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/alerts</code></p></td><td><p>Create an alert.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>PUT</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/alerts/{alertId}</code></p></td><td><p>Update an alert.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>DELETE</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/alerts/{alertId}</code></p></td><td><p>Delete an alert.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For more information, see [Custom Application Alerts and Notifications](custom-application-alerts).

## Schedules

Use these endpoints to view and update scheduled jobs for your CloudHub applications.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Method</th><th>Endpoint</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/applications/{domain}/schedules</code></p></td><td><p>List all schedules for an application.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>PUT</code></p></td><td><p><code>/applications/{domain}/schedules</code></p></td><td><p>Update all schedules for an application.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>PUT</code></p></td><td><p><code>/applications/{domain}/schedules/{scheduleId}</code></p></td><td><p>Update a specific schedule.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For more information, see [Manage Schedules](managing-schedules).

## Load Balancers

Use these endpoints to create, configure, and manage dedicated load balancers (DLBs) for your
CloudHub applications.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Method</th><th>Endpoint</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/loadbalancers</code></p></td><td><p>List load balancers in an organization.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs/{vpcId}/loadbalancers</code></p></td><td><p>Create a load balancer.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs/{vpcId}/loadbalancers/{lbId}</code></p></td><td><p>Get details for a specific load balancer.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>PATCH</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs/{vpcId}/loadbalancers/{lbId}</code></p></td><td><p>Update a load balancer (mapping rules, certificates, cipher suites).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>DELETE</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs/{vpcId}/loadbalancers/{lbId}</code></p></td><td><p>Delete a load balancer.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/loadbalancers/ciphersuites</code></p></td><td><p>List available cipher suites.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/logs/download</code></p></td><td><p>Initiate a DLB log download.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/logs/download/status</code></p></td><td><p>Check DLB log download status.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For detailed curl examples, see [Create a Load Balancer with the CloudHub API](lb-create-api),
[Certificate Validation in Dedicated Load Balancers](lb-cert-validation),
[Dedicated Load Balancer Mapping Rules](lb-mapping-rules), and
[Download Dedicated Load Balancer Logs](lb-download-logs).

## VPCs, VPNs, and Transit Gateways

Use these endpoints to manage Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs), Virtual Private Networks (VPNs), and
transit gateway attachments for your CloudHub network infrastructure.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Method</th><th>Endpoint</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs</code></p></td><td><p>List VPCs in an organization.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs</code></p></td><td><p>Create a VPC.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>PUT</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs/{vpcId}</code></p></td><td><p>Update a VPC (associated environments, firewall rules).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>DELETE</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs/{vpcId}</code></p></td><td><p>Remove a VPC.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs/{vpcId}/ipsec</code></p></td><td><p>Create a VPN connection.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>DELETE</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/vpcs/{vpcId}/ipsec/{vpnId}</code></p></td><td><p>Delete a VPN connection.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/tgws</code></p></td><td><p>List Transit Gateway attachments in an organization.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/tgws</code></p></td><td><p>Create a Transit Gateway attachment.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>PUT</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/tgws/{tgwId}</code></p></td><td><p>Update a Transit Gateway attachment.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>DELETE</code></p></td><td><p><code>/organizations/{orgId}/tgws/{tgwId}</code></p></td><td><p>Remove a Transit Gateway attachment.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For detailed curl examples, see [Create a VPC with the Anypoint Platform CLI](create-vpc-cli).

## Diagnostics

Use these endpoints to generate, retrieve, and analyze application diagnostics.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Method</th><th>Endpoint</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/instances/diagnostics</code></p></td><td><p>List diagnostics for an application.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/instances/{instanceId}/diagnostics</code></p></td><td><p>Get diagnostics for an instance without persisting them.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/instances/{instanceId}/diagnostics</code></p></td><td><p>Generate and persist diagnostics for an instance.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/instances/diagnostics/{diagnosticId}/download</code></p></td><td><p>Download a diagnostic.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/instances/diagnostics/analysis-readiness</code></p></td><td><p>Check if analysis can be triggered.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>GET</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/instances/{instanceId}/diagnostics/analysis/latest</code></p></td><td><p>Get the latest analysis for an instance.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>POST</code></p></td><td><p><code>/v2/applications/{domain}/instances/{instanceId}/diagnostics/analyze</code></p></td><td><p>Trigger an analysis for an instance.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

For more information, see
[Generate Apps Diagnostics and Analyze with Einstein](ch-diagnostics-agent).

## See Also

- [CloudHub API](cloudhub-api)
---

# CloudHub API

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The CloudHub management API (CloudHub API) enables you to programmatically access the functions of
Anypoint Runtime Manager, such as:

- Manage applications:
  - Create, deploy, start, stop, list, and delete an application on CloudHub.
  - Update application metadata, including the number of workers, Mule runtime engine version, and
    system properties.
  - Retrieve statistics for an application or worker.
  - Retrieve application transactions and events for a transaction.
  - Check for replay data, replay the flow for a transaction, and delete replay data for an
    application.
  - Manage static IP addresses and release unused IP addresses associated with an application.
  - Download the Mule package file for the application.
  - Update, enable, disable, or run all or some schedules.

- Manage logs, notifications, and alerts:
  - Add, update, or retrieve log levels for an application.
  - Download the log file for an application or instance.
  - Create, update, or retrieve a notification.
  - Mark a notification as read or unread.
  - Create, update, or delete an alert triggered by an application.
  - Retrieve an alert and alert history.

- Manage load balancers:
  - Create, update, or delete a load balancer.
  - List load balancers in an organization.
  - Test load balancer rules and return the number of the rule applied to the input.

- Manage VPCs, VPNs, and TGWs:
  - Create, update, list, and remove a VPC.
  - Create or delete a VPN connection.
  - Add, modify, and remove multiple AWS Transit Gateway attachments to your Anypoint Platform
    organization.

- Manage persistent queues:
  - Retrieve statistics for persistent queues.
  - Clear the entries from a persistent queue.

- Retrieve additional information about:
  - Supported Mule versions
  - The current organization, including usage statistics
  - Workers, including thread dumps and worker regions
  - Users, including permissions
  - Available domain names

For an interactive reference that includes supported resources, methods, required properties, and
expected responses, see the
[CloudHub API](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/cloudhub-api/).

The CloudHub API manages only applications deployed to CloudHub, the cloud-based version of Runtime
Manager. To manage on-premises applications using the API, see
[Runtime Manager REST Services](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/arm-rest-services).

## Use the CloudHub API

Before getting started, familiarize yourself with operations for applications.

You can use any HTTP client with the CloudHub API. With Java, use the
[Jersey client](https://jersey.github.io/) or
[HttpClient](https://hc.apache.org/httpclient-legacy/index.html) with
[Jackson](https://github.com/codehaus/jackson) for JSON support.

To access operations in the CloudHub API, you must:

1.  Generate an authorization bearer token (AUTH_BEARER_TOKEN) to authenticate with the API.

    To get the access token, you need the Anypoint Platform username and password of a user assigned
    the Organization Administrators role. See
    [Authentication](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/access-management-api/minor/1.0/pages/Authentication/)
    in the
    [Access Management API](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/access-management-api/minor/1.0/pages/home/).

    Your username specifies the environment to access. For example, if your username is `consuela`
    and the environment is `Development`, your username is `consuela@Development`. If you donât
    specify an environment, the API defaults to `Production`.

2.  Get the organization ID (ORG_ID).

    See the
    [`/api/me`](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/access-management-api/minor/1.0/console/method/%234250/)
    endpoint in the
    [Access Management API](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/access-management-api/minor/1.0/pages/home/).

3.  Get the environment ID (ENV_ID).

    See the
    [`/api/organizations/_ORG_ID_/environments`](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/access-management-api/minor/1.0/console/method/%234762/)
    endpoint in the
    [Access Management API](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/access-management-api/minor/1.0/pages/home/).

### Get a List of Applications

To get a list of apps deployed to CloudHub, run the following `curl` command:

curl -X GET \\ --url https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/cloudhub/api/applications \\ -H 'authorization:
Bearer AUTH_BEARER_TOKEN' \\ -H 'X-ANYPNT-ENV-ID: ENV_ID' \\ -H 'X-ANYPNT-ORG-ID: ORG_ID'

## Data Format

Resources and methods that return or accept a type use the JSON data format. Here is an example of
data received in JSON format in response to a request to get an application:

```json
{
  "versionId": "xxxxxxxx",
  "domain": "hello",
  "fullDomain": "hello.us-e1.cloudhub.io",
  "properties": {
    "foo":"bar"
  },
  "propertiesOptions": {},
  "status": "STARTED",
  "workers": {
    "type": {
      "name": "Micro",
      "weight": 0.1,
      "cpu": "0.1 vCores",
      "memory": "500 MB memory"
    },
    "amount": 1,
    "remainingOrgWorkers": 223.7,
    "totalOrgWorkers": 1000.0
  },
  "workerStatuses": [
    {
      "id": "i-xxxxxxxx",
      "host": "xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx",
      "port": 0,
      "status": "STARTED",
      "deployedRegion": "us-east-1",
      "staticIPEnabled": false
    }
  ],
  "lastUpdateTime": 1589868960908,
  "fileName": "hello.zip",
  "muleVersion": {
    "version": "4.3.0",
    "updateId": "xxxxxxxx",
    "latestUpdateId": "xxxxxxxx",
    "endOfSupportDate": 1633737600000
  },
  ...
}
```

## Rate Limits

The CloudHub management API enforces global usage limits, with some exceptions.

### Global Rate Limits for the CloudHub Management API

The following table lists the rate limits for the CloudHub management API endpoints except those
listed in [Exceptions to the Global Rate Limits](#exceptions-to-the-global-rate-limits).

When the app reaches the rate limit, CloudHub returns a 503 status code.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Endpoints</th><th>Control Plane</th><th>Rate Limit Per Client IP Address</th><th>Status Code</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td rowspan="3"><p>All except specified endpoints</p></td><td><p>U.S.</p></td><td><p>75 requests per second</p></td><td rowspan="3"><p><code>503</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>EU</p></td><td rowspan="2"><p>25 requests per second</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Government Cloud</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Exceptions to the Global Rate Limits

The following table lists the exceptions to the
[Global Rate Limits for the CloudHub Management API](#global-rate-limits-for-the-cloudhub-management-api).

When the app reaches the rate limit, CloudHub returns the status codes shown here.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Endpoints</th><th>Control Plane</th><th>Rate Limit Per Client IP Address</th><th>Status Code</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td colspan="4"><p><strong>Endpoints under</strong> <code>/api</code></p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="3"><p><code>/notifications</code></p></td><td><p>U.S.</p></td><td><p>75 requests per minute</p></td><td rowspan="3"><p><code>503</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>EU</p></td><td rowspan="2"><p>25 requests per minute</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Government Cloud</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="4"><p><strong>Endpoints under</strong> <code><strong>v2/applications/{domain}</strong></code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>/logs</code></p></td><td rowspan="4"><p>All</p></td><td rowspan="3"><p>10 requests per second</p></td><td rowspan="3"><p><code>429</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>/deployments/{deploymentId}/logs</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>/instances/{instanceId}/logs</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>/instances/{instanceId}/log-file</code></p></td><td><p>1 request per minute</p></td><td><p><code>500</code></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Status Codes and Error Handling

When you call the CloudHub API, the following status codes are returned:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Status Code</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>200</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The operation was successful.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>201</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The resource (such as an application) was created.</p></div><div><p>The <code>Location</code> header contains the location of the resource.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>404</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The resource was not found.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>409</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When creating a resource (such as a server, server group, or deployment), a resource with that name already exists.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>429</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The operation was unsuccessful due to reaching the rate limit.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>500</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The operation was unsuccessful.</p></div><div><p>See the HTTP body for details.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>503</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The operation was unsuccessful due to a temporary condition, such as server overload or reaching a rate limit.</p></div><div><p>Retry the operation.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

When errors occur, such as a 500 status code, the HTTP response contains a JSON response with an
error message:

```json
500
Content-Type: application/json
Server: Apache-Coyote/1.1
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2015 00:12:55 GMT

{
  message : "Some error message."
}
```

- [Access Management API](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/portals/anypoint-platform/f1e97bc6-315a-4490-82a7-23abe036327a.anypoint-platform/access-management-api/)
